title: Jaundice and RUQ tenderness with heavy drinking

chart.oneLiner: 50M with heavy alcohol use presents with 2 weeks of jaundice, right upper quadrant tenderness, anorexia, and low-grade fever.

opening: I've gone yellow, my belly's tender on the right, and I've had no appetite for weeks.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 50-year-old man, jaundiced, mildly ill. Good historian once asked.

HPI: 2 weeks of progressive jaundice, RUQ discomfort, nausea, anorexia, and low-grade fevers, following a recent heavy drinking binge on a background of years of daily alcohol use (only quantifies alcohol if asked). NO large acetaminophen use (verify). NO confusion at baseline (watch for encephalopathy).
PMH: alcohol use disorder; possible early cirrhosis. MEDS: none regularly; occasional acetaminophen (confirm). SOCIAL: drinks a pint of spirits daily for years.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: jaundiced, ill-appearing.
- Vitals: BP 126/78, HR 98, RR 16, 38.0°C, SpO2 98% RA.
- Abdomen: tender hepatomegaly; possible ascites (shifting dullness) if examined; spider angiomata/palmar erythema if examined.
- Neuro: alert; check for asterixis (encephalopathy) if examined.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the quantity/duration of alcohol use or acetaminophen use unless asked about alcohol or medications.
- Stigmata of chronic liver disease and asterixis are revealed only if examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.LFTs: AST 240, ALT 95 — AST:ALT ratio >2:1 (typical of alcohol); both usually <300–400. GGT high.
labs.Severity: Maddrey discriminant function / MELD calculated to grade severity.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Left axis deviation with qR in aVL and rS in inferior leads; normal QRS duration.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Left axis deviation with qR in aVL and rS in inferior leads; normal QRS duration. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.clinicalVignette: A 62-year-old man presenting with palpitations has a tracing showing left axis deviation consistent with left anterior fascicular block.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Borderline cardiomegaly with an unfolded thoracic aorta; lungs clear.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Borderline cardiomegaly with an unfolded thoracic aorta; lungs clear. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph borderline cardiomegaly unfolded aorta
cxr.clinicalVignette: A 67-year-old man with longstanding hypertension has a film showing borderline cardiomegaly and an unfolded thoracic aorta.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Alcoholic hepatitis (leading,7). Acute viral hepatitis (CANT-MISS to exclude,4). Acetaminophen injury (CANT-MISS,3). Biliary obstruction/cholangitis (CANT-MISS,3). Ischemic hepatitis (core,2). Decompensated cirrhosis (core,3). Names alcoholic hepatitis with the AST:ALT >2:1 pattern in context of heavy alcohol use (core,3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Alcoholic hepatitis. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Acute viral hepatitis; Acetaminophen injury; Biliary obstruction/cholangitis. Also reasonable: Ischemic hepatitis; Decompensated cirrhosis.

coreActions.item: Names alcoholic hepatitis with the AST:ALT >2:1 pattern in context of heavy alcohol use

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: LFTs incl. AST:ALT ratio and GGT (CANT-MISS,3). Bilirubin (2). INR and albumin — synthetic function (3). CBC (1). Acetaminophen level (CANT-MISS,2). Viral hepatitis serologies (2). RUQ ultrasound (1). Calculate severity — Maddrey/MELD (1). PENALTY: not excluding viral/acetaminophen causes (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; LFTs; Bilirubin; INR; CBC; Acetaminophen; Viral serologies; Severity.

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = left anterior fascicular block (marked left axis deviation, qR in aVL, rS inferiorly, near-normal QRS width). CREDIT: systematic rate & rhythm read (6); identifies the marked left axis deviation (4); recognizes left anterior fascicular block (3); distinguishes it from inferior MI (2). PENALTY: calling the study normal / missing the abnormality (-3).
keyFindings: qR in aVL with rS inferiorly
commonMistakes: attributing the left axis to inferior MI instead of LAFB
steps.idealAnswer: Left anterior fascicular block: left axis deviation, qR in aVL, rS in the inferior leads, near-normal QRS width.

coreActions: qR in aVL with rS inferiorly

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = borderline cardiomegaly with an unfolded thoracic aorta; lungs clear. CREDIT: notes borderline cardiomegaly (6); notes the unfolded aorta as a chronic change (4). PENALTY: calling the study normal / missing the abnormality (-3).
keyFindings: notes the unfolded aorta as chronic
criticalFindings: Borderline cardiomegaly with an unfolded aorta.
commonMistakes: over-calling borderline heart size on a portable film
steps.idealAnswer: Borderline cardiomegaly with an unfolded thoracic aorta — chronic changes, lungs clear.

criticalActions: Borderline cardiomegaly with an unfolded aorta.
coreActions: notes the unfolded aorta as chronic

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: AST:ALT >2:1, high bilirubin, prolonged INR, negative viral/APAP workup, heavy alcohol use. What is your diagnosis and how do you grade severity?
steps.rubric: Alcoholic hepatitis (6). Grades severity with the Maddrey discriminant function (or MELD) to guide steroid use (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Alcoholic hepatitis. Grade severity with the Maddrey discriminant function (or MELD) to guide corticosteroid use (DF >=32 indicates severe disease).

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH acute liver injury (alcoholic hepatitis): alcohol cessation + supportive care/nutrition (CANT-MISS,6); thiamine/vitamins + manage withdrawal (4); risk-stratify with discriminant function/MELD (3); corticosteroids if severe (DF≥32) after excluding infection/bleed (4); screen for and treat infection (3); hepatology consult (2). PENALTY: steroids with untreated infection/active GI bleed (-3); ongoing alcohol/hepatotoxins (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Alcohol cessation and supportive care: nutrition (high protein/calorie), thiamine and vitamins, treat the alcohol withdrawal, and manage volume/electrolytes. Calculate discriminant function/MELD; if severe (DF ≥32), corticosteroids (prednisolone) after excluding active infection/GI bleed, reassess with Lille score. Screen for and treat infection. Hepatology consult; avoid further hepatotoxins.

penalties.item: Steroids with untreated infection or GI bleed

unsafeActions: Steroids with untreated infection or GI bleed

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 50M with heavy alcohol use presents with 2 weeks of jaundice, right upper quadrant tenderness, anorexia, and low-grade fever. The diagnosis is Alcoholic hepatitis, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: tender hepatomegaly; possible ascites (shifting dullness) if examined; spider angiomata/palmar erythema if examined.
Neuro: alert; check for asterixis (encephalopathy) if examined.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Alcoholic hepatitis while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Alcoholic hepatitis and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
commonPitfalls: Steroids with untreated infection or GI bleed
criticalInterpretationItems: Borderline cardiomegaly with an unfolded aorta.
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Borderline cardiomegaly with an unfolded aorta.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Borderline cardiomegaly with an unfolded aorta.
imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
